CWGroup.CW: Notification laying down proceedings to be undertaken by faceless assessment unit
Show-cause notice issued by jurisdictional Assessing Officer instead of faceless assessment unit
Notice under section 148 to be issued by faceless assessment unit
Central Board of Direct Taxes Notification No. 18 of 2022/F. No. 370142/16/2022-TPL (Part 1), dated 29-3-20222.

HeadNote.Para: For the assessment year 2019-20, a show-cause notice was issued under section 148A(b) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer on March 2, 2023 calling upon the assessee to show cause why notice under section 148 should not be issued on the basis of information that -income chargeable to tax had escaped assessment. After the assessee’s submission, order under section 148A(d) was passed on March 30, 2023 and on the same date, notice under section 148 was issued by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer. The Commissioner (Appeals) affirmed the reassessment. On appeal to the -Tribunal raising an additional ground that the reassessment proceedings were invalid having been undertaken by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer and not by the faceless assessment unit, as required by notification dated March 29, 20221 :

Held, allowing the appeal, that the notice under section 148 was issued by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer and not by the faceless assessment unit, almost a year after the issuance of the notification by the Ministry of Finance, Government of India on March 29, 2022 requiring issuance of notice and conduct of assessment proceedings by the faceless assessment unit. There was thus a clear breach of the binding notification issued under section 144B of the Act requiring issuance of notices and conduct of proceedings through the faceless assessment unit. Thus, the notice issued was without jurisdiction and bad in law. (para 10)

JDNotice.JDN: Dy. CIT v. Aman Batra 2025 SCC OnLine ITAT 6348 , Bagga Singh v. Dy. CIT (International Taxation) 2025 SCC OnLine ITAT 6284, Jatinder Singh Bhangu v. Union of India ; 2024 SCC OnLine P&H 9337 and Jasjit Singh v. Union of India ; 2024 SCC OnLine P&H 9677 followed.

Para.Italics: Chronological list of cases cited on page(s)

Referred.CRT: 2025 SCC OnLine ITAT 6348, CIT (Dy.) v. Aman Batra 757f, 759g
2025 SCC OnLine ITAT 6284, Bagga Singh v. Dy. CIT (International Taxation) 759c, g
; 2024 SCC OnLine P&H 9677, Jasjit Singh v. Union of India 758e-f
; 2024 SCC OnLine P&H 9337 , Jatinder Singh Bhangu v. Union of India 756f, 758d-e, f

CounselGroup.forApellant: Ms. Pallavi and Sudhir Sehgal, Advocates, for the assessee.
CounselGroup.forRespondent: Vivek Vardhan, Additional Commissioner of Income-tax, Senior -Departmental representative, for the Department.

JudgmentGroup.Title: Order
JudgmentGroup.Para: Vikram Singh Yadav .—This is an appeal filed by the assessee against the order of the learned Commissioner of Income-tax (Appeals)/National Faceless Appeal Centre, Delhi dated March 18, 2025 pertaining to the assessment year 2019-20.
2. At the outset, the learned authorised representative submitted that the assessee has moved a prayer for admission of the following additional ground of appeal :

Quote.Para2:
“Notwithstanding the original grounds of appeal, the reassessment proceedings initiated under section 148 of the Income-tax Act, 1961 are bad in law and void ab initio, as the notice under section 148 was issued by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer (JAO) instead of the National Faceless Assessment Centre (NFAC), which is in violation of the mandatory procedure prescribed under sections 144B and 151A of the Act, vide notification Central Board of Direct Taxes Notification No. 18/2022/F. No. 370142/16/2022-TPL (Part 1) dated March 29, 2022 ([2022] 442 ITR (St.) 198) and as clarified by the hon’ble Punjab and Haryana High Court in the case of
Jatinder Singh Bhangu v. Union of India
3 (W. P. No. 15745 of 2024, dated July 19, 2024).”

JudgmentGroup.Para:
3. It was submitted that all the facts are borne out from the order of the Assessing Officer and no fresh facts are to be investigated and the additional ground being purely a legal ground, the same may be admitted in light of the decision of the hon’ble Supreme Court in the case of
National Thermal Power Co. Ltd. v. CIT
4.
4. The learned Departmental representative has been heard who has not raised any specific objection for admission of the additional ground of appeal.

5. The additional ground so sought to be raised being purely legal in nature, the same is hereby admitted for necessary adjudication following the decision of the hon’ble Supreme Court in the case of
National Thermal Power Co. Ltd. v. CIT
5

6. In this regard, the learned authorised representative further submitted that basis certain information that the assessee has purchased certain immovable property and has made cash payment of Rs. 40,00,000, a show cause was issued under section 148A(b) of the Act by the Income-tax Officer, Ward-1, Nabha on March 2, 2023 as to why notice under section 148 should not be issued on the basis of information that income chargeable to tax has escaped assessment. In response, the assessee filed her submission and thereafter, an order under section 148A(d) was passed on March 30, 2023 and on the same date, i. e., March 30, 2022, notice under section 148 was issued by Income-tax Officer, Ward, Nabha being the jurisdictional Assessing Officer.

7. It was submitted that the notice so issued under section 148 was in clear contravention of the mandatory requirement of faceless proceedings stipulated under the Income-tax Act post issuance of notification by the Ministry of Finance, Government of India dated March 29, 2022 under section 144B of the Act. It was submitted that after issuance of the said notification on March 29, 2022, the notice under section 148 ought to have been issued by the Faceless Assessment Unit and not by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer. It was accordingly submitted that the notice so issued under section 148 has clearly been issued without jurisdiction. In this regard, our reference was drawn to the decision of co-ordinate Bench in the case of
Dy. CIT v. Aman Batra
6 (I. T. A. No. 1040/Chand/2024 and C. O. No. 41/Chand/2024, dated May 13, 2025), wherein the co-ordinate Bench following the decision of hon’ble Punjab and Haryana High Court has held that the notice issued under section 148 is bad in the eyes of law. In this regard, the relevant finding of the co-ordinate Bench read as under :

Quote.Para2: “6. We have duly considered the rival contentions and gone through the record carefully. Admittedly, notice under section 148 was issued after the notification issued by the Ministry of Finance, Government of India on March 29, 2022. The notice under section 148 has been issued on March 29, 2023, i. e., almost one year from the notification. Thus, facts of other year are squarely applicable. Theissue in dispute is covered by the judgment of the hon’ble jurisdictional High Court which read as under :
Quote.Para3: ‘1. Challenge made through the instant petition is to the notice dated March 1, 2025 (annexure P1) issued to the petitioner by the respondents under section 148 of the Income-tax Act, 1961. The primary ground of challenge raised by the petitioner is that the impugned notice has been issued by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer which could not have been done because in terms of the notification dated March 29, 2022 (annexure P2), issued by the Ministry of Finance, Government of India, the impugned notice could have been issued only by way of faceless assessment.
2. In support of his afore submission, the learned counsel for the petitioner places reliance on the following two judgments of this court :
Quote.Para4:
(i) C. W. P. No. 15745 of 2024, titled
Jatinder Singh Bhangu v. Union of India
7, decided on July 19, 2024 ; and

(ii) C. W. P. No. 21509 of 2023, titled
Jasjit Singh v. Union of India
8, decided on July 29, 2024.
Quote.Para3:
3. The learned counsel for the respondents does not dispute the fact that the case of the petitioner is covered in his favour by the law laid down through the aforesaid two judgments rendered by two -different co-ordinate Benches of this court in
Jatinder Singh Bhangu v. Union of India
1 and
Jasjit Singh v. Union of India
2.

4. In the light of the above, in terms of the law laid down in
Jatinder Singh Bhangu v. Union of India
1 and
Jasjit Singh v. Union of India
2 the impugned notice dated March 1, 2025 (annexure P1) issued by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer, is hereby quashed with liberty to the respondents to proceed against the petitioner in accordance with law.
Quote.Para2: 5. The petition is allowed in the above terms.’

7. Respectfully following the judgment of the hon’ble High Court, we allow the ground of appeal of cross-objection and hold that notice issued under section 148 of the Income-tax Act in the assessment year 2019-20 is bad in the eyes of law. It was without jurisdiction. Accordingly, reassessment order is quashed.

8. Though the Revenue has raised a number of issues on the merits in its appeal, but we have quashed the reassessment order, therefore, all the issues become academic in nature at this stage. We have quashed the assessment order on the strength of number of -judgments rendered by the hon’ble jurisdictional High Court, which is a view unanimously taken by other hon’ble High Courts also. Therefore, we do not deem it necessary to consider other issues on the merits.”

JudgmentGroup.Para:
8. Further, reference was drawn to another decision of a co-ordinate Bench in the case of
Bagga Singh v. Dy. CIT
9, in I. T. A. No. 2318/Chand/2024, dated May 13, 2025 wherein similar view has been taken by the co-ordinate Chandigarh Benches.

9. The learned senior Departmental representative is heard who has fairly submitted that though there are decisions of the hon’ble Punjab and Haryana High Court, however, the Revenue is in appeal before the hon’ble Supreme Court against such decisions rendered by the hon’ble High Court. However, the learned senior Departmental representative has fairly -submitted that there is no stay which has been granted by the hon’ble Supreme Court against the decision rendered by the hon’ble Punjab and Haryana High Court. Further, he has relied on the orders of the lower authorities.

10. I have heard the rival contentions and perused the material available on the record. In the instant case, it is noted that the notice under section 148 has been issued on March 30, 2023 by the Income-tax Officer, Ward, Nabha who is the jurisdictional Assessing Officer and not by the Faceless Assessment Unit. The said notice has been issued almost after a year from the issuance of notification by the Ministry of Finance, Government of India on March 29, 2022 requiring issuance of notice and conduct of assessment proceedings by the Faceless Assessment Unit. There is thus a clear breach of the binding notification so issued under section 144B requiring issuance of notices and conduct of proceedings through the Faceless Assessment Unit. The co-ordinate Bench in the case of
Dy. CIT v. Aman Batra
10 and thereafter in case of
Bagga Singh v. Dy. CIT (International Taxation)
9has referred to the decision of the hon’ble jurisdictional High Court wherein the notice issued by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer has been quashed by the hon’ble Punjab and Haryana High Court and by following the same, it has been held that the notice so issued is bad in law. Nothing has been brought on record to deviate from the view so takenby the co-ordinate Benches which in turn are guided by the decision of the hon’ble jurisdictional High Court. Respectfully following the -decision of hon’ble jurisdictional High Court which has been followed by the co-ordinate Benches in the decisions referred supra, the notice so issued on March 30, 2023 by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer, i. e., Income-tax Officer, Ward Nabha is clearly without jurisdiction and bad in law. In light of the same, the additional ground of appeal so taken by the assessee is hereby allowed.
11. In light of the aforesaid, the other grounds of appeal have become academic in nature and the same are left open and not adjudicated upon.
12. In the result, the appeal of the assessee is allowed.
Order pronounced in the open court on August 18, 2025.
